title: Spring Spirits
alt_text: A series of botanical character illustrations depicting seasonal spirits in warm duotone colours
description: A personal series exploring the intersection of botanical illustration and mythological character design. Each figure embodies a specific plant genus — their forms, moods, and palettes driven by the plant's real ecology.
story: This series began as a sketchbook exploration: what would it look like if plants took humanoid form? I drew on the Persephone myth as a structural reference — the idea of beings whose identity is literally rooted in a place, a season, a cycle.

Each character went through multiple rounds of thumbnail sketches, value studies in greyscale, and colour explorations before final rendering in Procreate. I limited each piece to a three-colour palette to force cohesion across the series.
The biggest challenge was balancing botanical accuracy with expressive character design. I spent a lot of time with reference photographs and botanical diagrams before any mark-making.
role: Solo artist — concept, illustration, colour direction

title: Underworld Dreams
alt_text: Dark atmospheric digital paintings of mythological underworld environments — Asphodel, Elysium, and Tartarus
description: A series of atmospheric environment illustrations reimagining the Greek underworld's distinct realms through a contemporary visual lens.
story: Inspired by scattered descriptions across ancient texts — Homer, Virgil, Pindar — this series asks: what would each realm actually feel like to inhabit? Not as stage dressing for a hero's journey, but as a place.

Asphodel is grey and flat, fog at knee height, indistinct outlines. Elysium is almost unbearably bright, the light slightly wrong, like a memory of summer rather than summer itself. Tartarus is structural — walls and angles, fire that doesn't flicker.

The challenge was sustaining a cohesive visual language across three pieces with radically different moods. I used a shared edge treatment — soft, slightly luminous outlines — to keep them in the same world.

title: Myth & Mind
alt_text: Pages from Myth & Mind — a sequential art comic exploring Jungian archetypes through Greek mythology
description: An ongoing comic series mapping Jungian archetypes to ancient Greek myths. The visual language merges classical fresco aesthetics with clean flat graphic design.
story: Myth & Mind started as a thesis question: if Jung drew from Greek myth to build his archetypes, what happens when you reverse the process and read the myths through the archetypes?

Each issue pairs a mythological figure with an archetypal concept. Issue 1 covers the Hero and Theseus; Issue 2 unpacks the Trickster through Hermes. The visual style uses flat fills with ornamental line detail, referencing Ancient Greek vase painting without directly copying it.
I write and illustrate every page solo, which makes the process slow but keeps the voice unified. The letttering is hand-drawn and digitally refined.

title: Divine Fragments
alt_text: Cover and interior pages of Divine Fragments, a self-published anthology of short myth-retelling comics
description: A self-published anthology collecting eight short comic stories, each retelling the myth of a minor deity or nymph who rarely receives their own narrative.
story: Divine Fragments came from frustration with how anthology comics tend to flatten the contributions — everyone writes in the same general style and the whole thing reads as one bland voice. I wanted to do the opposite: let each story have a totally distinct look.

The eight stories range from a two-page silent piece about Lethe (the river of forgetfulness) to a twelve-page story about the nymph Syrinx. Each uses a different palette and linework approach — some are painterly, some are stark graphic.
Self-publishing meant I handled everything: writing, illustration, cover design, formatting for print, and distribution. It sold out its first print run of 50 copies through Instagram direct orders.

title: Olympus Observed
alt_text: Article header for Olympus Observed — a Medium essay series on Greek mythology in contemporary visual media
description: An ongoing Medium essay series analysing how Greek mythology is reinterpreted across contemporary graphic novels, animation, and digital illustration.
story: This series grew from a simple observation: mythology has never left popular culture, but the ways we retell it keep shifting. I wanted to write criticism that took the visual dimension of modern myth-retellings seriously — not just the story choices, but the drawing style, the palette, the page architecture.
Each essay builds a close reading of a single work alongside its mythological source, then widens out to a cultural argument. I try to keep the language accessible without dumbing the analysis down.
Published on Medium with a small but engaged reader base. The most widely read piece reached [X] reads — placeholder pending analytics access.

title: Between Worlds
alt_text: Decorative header for Between Worlds — a personal essay collection about art and digital identity
description: A five-part personal essay collection exploring what it means to be a visual artist navigating text-heavy digital spaces.
story: Between Worlds started from a specific irritation: the internet gives visual artists enormous reach, but most of the tools — captions, bios, threads — are still deeply text-centric. What does it do to the work to translate it into words constantly?

The five essays each take a specific artifact as their anchor — a single Instagram post, a comic page, a piece of fan mail — and use it to open up a larger question about audience, authorship, and the gap between intention and reception.
I wrote these over about eight months, going back and forth between long draft sessions and letting them breathe. They're the most personal writing I've published.

title: Flora & Co. Instagram Strategy
alt_text: Instagram grid mockup and content asset examples for Flora & Co. botanical lifestyle brand
description: End-to-end social media content strategy and creation for a new botanical lifestyle brand — from voice guide to 90-day execution.
story: Flora & Co. came to me with a beautiful product and no idea how to talk about it online. I built their content strategy from scratch: audience definition, content pillars, visual identity guidelines for social, and a 90-day editorial calendar.

I created all graphic assets, wrote every caption, and managed community engagement throughout the campaign. The illustrated content I produced — botanical how-to carousels and educational posts — consistently outperformed photography by 3×.
By the end of the 90-day engagement, the account had grown by 2,400 followers, with an average save rate of 380 per post and a sustained 6.2% engagement rate.
role: Social Media Manager, Content Writer, Graphic Designer

title: Luna Collective
alt_text: Content grid examples and story templates for Luna Collective wellness brand
description: Ongoing social content strategy and execution for an independent wellness brand focused on moon cycle practices.
story: Luna Collective is an ongoing relationship — I built their content strategy from audience research and have managed their social presence month-to-month since 2023.

The brand's audience is deeply engaged but expects consistency and authenticity. I developed a content framework around the lunar calendar itself: every post cycle maps to a phase, which means planning happens in 28-day rhythms rather than standard monthly blocks.
Results are under NDA, but the account has sustained measurable growth and above-average saves rates throughout our partnership.

title: Myth in Motion
alt_text: Screenshot of Myth in Motion — an interactive Rive animation telling the Persephone myth through clickable states
description: An interactive Rive piece letting viewers navigate the myth of Persephone through clickable scene transitions and branching story states.
story: Myth in Motion was my first serious attempt at interactive narrative design. The goal was simple: take a myth people think they know and let them move through it at their own pace, making small choices that reveal different details.

The state machine required planning every possible user path before touching a single animation. I mapped it on paper first — entrance states, hover states, click transitions, exit loops — and the Rive build was actually faster because of it.
The piece taught me that interactive animation is fundamentally different from sequential illustration. Every frame needs to make sense as a held state, not just a moment in a transition.

title: Petal Pulse
alt_text: Animated botanical illustration from Petal Pulse — Rive ambient interactive piece
description: A looping ambient animation of botanical forms that respond to mouse position — colour and scale shift softly as you move through the canvas.
story: Petal Pulse grew from curiosity about Rive's listener events. I wanted to make something that felt alive without demanding attention — ambient interaction, not a game.

Mouse position drives subtle colour shifts (cool to warm) and scale changes across a layered botanical composition. The transitions are slow enough that some people don't immediately realise the piece is responding to them.
That delayed realisation is the whole point. It rewards sustained attention without requiring it.

title: Roots Review
alt_text: Editorial illustration spreads created for Roots Review — an independent literary magazine on nature and ecology
description: Three full-spread editorial illustrations commissioned for Roots Review, an independent literary magazine focused on nature writing and ecology.
story: Roots Review publishes long essays about place, ecology, and the more-than-human world. The editor reached out after seeing the botanical work in my Spring Spirits series.

Each illustration accompanies a specific essay, so the brief is text-first: read the essay deeply, find the image that lives inside it. For one piece about rewilding, I illustrated the tension between managed woodland and wild growth through two contrasting plant forms sharing one frame.
I work closely with editors on these — it's genuinely collaborative, not a handoff after a brief. That's the kind of editorial work I find most interesting.

title: Celestial Quarterly
alt_text: Illustrated editorial spreads for Celestial Quarterly — a science communication magazine covering astronomy
description: Ongoing quarterly illustration series for a science communication magazine, translating complex astronomical concepts into accessible visual narratives.
story: Each quarter I deliver two full-page illustrations for Celestial Quarterly. The challenge is balancing scientific accuracy with visual storytelling for a general-audience publication — the editors check the science, I push for the feeling.

My favourite commission so far was a piece on the Oort Cloud: a vast, cold, barely-understood shell of icy bodies at the edge of the solar system. Representing 'vast and barely understood' visually without resorting to generic space imagery required several rounds of concept sketches.
Editorial illustration keeps me honest. Deadlines are real, briefs are specific, and there's no room for indulgent style experiments when a 3,000-word article is waiting.
